title: Monitoring Generative Systems
concept: Teams track generation latency, failure rates, and user feedback signals to catch quality regressions or emerging misuse patterns in production.

exercises.prompt: Why do development teams monitor AI systems after they are deployed to the public?

options.text: To catch errors, slowness, and bad outputs in real time
To make the AI write longer stories automatically
To change the background color of the app daily

exercises.explain: Monitoring live systems ensures they remain fast, correct, and safe for real users after they are launched.

cards.title: Who's Watching the AI?
cards.body: Once you deploy a cool AI model to the world, how do you know it's actually behaving? You can't sit and read millions of chats yourself. Let's learn how teams keep an eye on live AI systems automatically!

cards.title: What is Generative Monitoring?
cards.body: Monitoring Generative Systems means continuously tracking live performance data—like how long it takes to generate text (latency), how often the system crashes (failure rate), and how users react (like clicking thumbs-down). This helps teams catch broken models or bad behaviors before they affect everyone.

cards.title: The Lemonade Stand Monitor
cards.body: Imagine running a lemonade stand. If you only look at your total cash at the end of the day, you won't know if customers waited 20 minutes in line, or if some cups were served warm. Monitoring a generative system is like having helper birds watch the line length, taste-test the drinks, and listen to customer whispers in real time so you can fix issues instantly.

cards.title: Catching a Slow Bot
cards.body: An AI customer service bot starts taking 15 seconds to reply instead of 2 seconds (high latency). At the same time, users start clicking the 'thumbs-down' button rapidly. A monitoring dashboard flags this immediately, alerting the engineers to roll back to a faster, working version of the model.

cards.title: Myth: Once Built, Always Perfect
cards.body: Many people think that once an AI model is trained and tested, it will always work perfectly. In reality, real-world users ask unexpected questions, servers get overloaded, and models can suddenly start outputting nonsense. Continuous monitoring is always required!

cards.title: Blind vs. Monitored AI
cards.body: Comparing blind deployment with active monitoring. Keeping track of metrics keeps your system healthy and users happy!

right.text: Real-time charts show latency, error rates, and user flags, letting you fix bugs before users even notice.

left.text: No tracking. You have no idea if the AI is slow, broken, or offending users until they stop using your app.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is a direct user feedback signal that helps detect poor AI generations?

options.text: The color theme of the website dashboard
A user clicking a 'thumbs-down' button on a response
The time of day the user logged in
The size of the database storage

exercises.explain: User feedback signals like thumbs-down buttons or report flags are direct indicators that the AI is generating low-quality or incorrect answers.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Once an AI system is launched, we no longer need to monitor it because the model does not change.
exercises.explain: Monitoring is an ongoing process because new user prompts, system updates, and server loads constantly change how the AI behaves in production.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the statement about AI speed.
exercises.sentence: When we measure how long an AI takes to respond, we are tracking its ___________.
exercises.explain: Latency measures the time delay between a user's prompt and the AI's generated response. Keeping this low keeps users happy!

exercises.prompt: An automated monitor flags broken patterns. Which of these AI outputs shows a clear generation failure that needs to be caught?

options.text: Prompt: "Tell me a joke." -> Response: "Why did the scarecrow win an award? Because he was outstanding in his field!"
Prompt: "Say hello." -> Response: "hello hello hello hello hello hello hello hello hello hello hello..."
Prompt: "What is 2+2?" -> Response: "2 + 2 is equal to 4."

exercises.explain: AI generated response B is a repetitive loop ('hello hello hello hello...'), which is a classic failure mode that automated monitoring systems should flag.

exercises.prompt: Your team's AI writing assistant is suddenly acting slow and giving strange answers. Which set of metrics will help you find and fix this issue?

options.text: How many total visits your website gets per day
Generation latency, system errors, and user feedback flags
The font style used in the chat window
The physical weight of the server racks

exercises.explain: Total traffic only tells you how popular your app is, not whether it is failing, generating bad responses, or running extremely slowly. You must track latency, errors, and user feedback signals to measure quality!
